exemplar.title: To What Extent Does Aerobic Exercise Influence Memory During Adolescence?

detailedExemplarData.title: To What Extent Does Aerobic Exercise Influence Memory During Adolescence?

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Topic is communicated accurately and consistently linked to purpose throughout.
Research question is clearly stated and prominently placed.
Effective use of section headings and definitions (e.g., BDNF, memory consolidation) maintains focus.
weaknesses: Methodology lacks an explicit search strategy (databases, keywords, inclusion/exclusion).
Scope of âmemoryâ is broad, shifting between types without always distinguishing them.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two of the three strands (topic communication and research question) clearly sit in the 5â6 band, while methodology sits at the top of the 3â4 band. On balance, the work demonstrates sustained focus and method but could sharpen its methodological transparency and narrow its operational definitions, warranting a mid-band mark of 5.

strengths: Demonstrates solid subject knowledge of neuroplasticity, sleep stages and genetic factors with accurate application of primary sources.
Uses appropriate terminology (e.g., SWS, VOâmax, internal validity) in context.
Empirical results are correctly interpreted and linked to the argument.
weaknesses: Occasional drift into unsubstantiated general health claims.
APA citation style is inconsistently applied, and some theoretical models receive only brief mention.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Both strands fall clearly in the 3â4 band: knowledge is good and terminology mostly accurate, but occasional conceptual drift and citation inconsistencies prevent the highest band. A top-of-band mark of 4 reflects this performance.

strengths: Presents balanced and relevant research, critiquing sample sizes, design and generalisability.
Analysis links mechanisms (BDNF, sleep architecture) to memory consolidation with supported conclusions.
Discussion synthesises environmental and genetic influences, acknowledging natureânurture interplay.
weaknesses: Depth is unevenâstatistical effect sizes and p-values are omitted.
Causal language occasionally overstates correlational findings; integration of divergent designs is limited.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strands receive 7 (research), 5 (analysis) and 5 (evaluation), placing the work in the 4â6 band overall. The essay offers clear analysis and partial critical evaluation, but uneven depth and overreach in interpretation suggest a mid-band mark of 5.

strengths: Follows recognised EE conventions: title page, word count, table of contents and labeled sections.
Logical ordering of sections enhances readability and navigation.
weaknesses: Table of contents lacks leader dots and clear indentation for sub-headings.
Dense block quotations and lengthy paraphrases could be streamlined for clarity.
Suggested conceptual diagram is missing, which would aid reader engagement.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All core structural and formatting conventions are met, placing presentation in the 3â4 band. However, minor layout and readability issues (TOC hierarchy, block quotations) prevent the top mark, yielding 3.

strandSelections.reasoning:
Key psychological terms (neuroplasticity, slow-wave activity, theta rhythms) and research concepts (internal validity, independent-measures design, heritability) are used mostly accurately and in appropriate contexts.  Citations accompany definitions, and statistical or methodological vocabulary (VOâR, ELISA) is employed correctly.

A few inaccuracies emerge â equating cortisol change directly with stress reduction, or conflating slow oscillations with all memory types â and APA style is inconsistent, preventing full fluency.  

Hence the work fits comfortably in the âmostly accurateâ 3-4 band, meriting 4 marks.

Formatting is broadly consistent: word count, title and table of contents are provided; headings use bold and hierarchical numbering; and in-text citations plus a works-cited list follow a recognisable (though not flawless) MLA style. Page numbers are indicated in the table but not visible in the excerpt; assuming they are present in the full document, layout aligns with EE expectations.

Because most layout conventions are observed and enhance clarity, the work earns the top band (3-4) with 4 marks, acknowledging minor citation inconsistencies.

The essay demonstrates solid command of psychological content: BDNFâs role in neuroplasticity, NREM/REM distinctions, VOâmax, and heritability are explained accurately and linked to adolescent cognition.  Empirical results are summarised correctly and applied to support or challenge the central argument.  

Yet discussion sometimes drifts into general health claims (e.g., social-media-driven exercise trends) without rigorous scholarly backing, and broader theoretical models (e.g., Baddeley & Hitch, synaptic homeostasis) are only briefly referenced.  

Overall knowledge is good but not consistently sophisticated, matching the 3-4 band at its top end (4).

For a literature-based EE, methodology rests on the selection and critical reading of sources.  The student draws from primary empirical papers (e.g., Jeon 2015, Neikrug 2021, Mollon 2021) and specialist reviews, showing a generally relevant range that covers biological, behavioural and genetic perspectives.  Source choice appears purposeful (original data with adolescents, links to BDNF and sleep) and the reliability/validity of each study is commented on.

However, the strategy for searching, inclusion/exclusion, or evaluating evidence quality is not explicitly outlined, and reliance on a small number of Ã©tudes (two Jeon papers) leaves some gaps (e.g., female samples, longitudinal data).  

This mostly complete yet not fully systematic approach corresponds with the upper end of the 3-4 band; 4 marks are awarded.

The student states the topic â the effect of aerobic exercise on memory in adolescence â explicitly in the title and reiterates it in the introduction, explaining why adolescence is a sensitive developmental window and why memory is of practical concern. The purpose (to evaluate the extent of influence) is clearly articulated and underpins the logical structure that follows (claims, counter-claims, discussion, conclusion).  

â¢ Key terms such as âaerobic exercise,â âBDNF,â and âmemory consolidationâ are defined early, showing clarity for a non-expert reader.
â¢ The table of contents and section headings maintain this focus consistently to the end of the essay.  

Because communication is accurate, sustained and explicitly linked to the stated purpose throughout, the work fits the 5-6 band; there are no significant lapses, so the upper mark in the band (6) is appropriate.

Evidence is largely contemporary, peer-reviewed and directly relevant to adolescents (Jeon 2015; Neikrug 2021; Mollon 2021).  Supporting and counter evidence are balanced and sources are critiqued for sample size, design and generalisability, indicating purposeful selection.  

Nevertheless, breadth is moderateâseveral sections depend on single studies, females are under-represented, and no meta-analytic findings on sleep or exercise intensity are introduced.  These shortcomings prevent placement in the very top band.  

Thus the strand satisfies the descriptors for 7-9, with a conservative score of 7.

The research question â âTo what extent does aerobic exercise influence memory during adolescence?â â is concise, focused and placed prominently.  The essay remains tied to this question by organising evidence into mechanistic claims (BDNF, sleep) and counter-points (exercise intensity, genetics).  

Minor limitations reduce precision slightly: âmemoryâ is treated generally, shifting between working, declarative and procedural memory without always distinguishing them, and âaerobic exerciseâ spans a wide intensity range.  

Given this small but noticeable breadth, the strand meets the 5-6 band but with a mid-band mark (5) rather than the maximum.

The essay follows recognised EE conventions: title page with word count, abstract-style introduction, clearly labelled sections, discussion, conclusion and works-cited list. The internal headings mirror the table of contents, enabling reader navigation and maintaining logical flow.

All essential structural elements are present and appropriately ordered; any minor issues (lengthy block quotations, extended paraphrase) do not impede readability. This fulfils the highest band for structure (3-4), warranting 4 marks.

The discussion section attempts synthesis, acknowledging natureânurture interplay and limitations across methodologies, and the conclusion relates back to the research question.  The student evaluates strengths and weaknesses (internal validity vs. generalisability) and suggests practical applications.

Critical evaluation remains partial: genetic factors are asserted to âovershadowâ exercise without reconciliation of effect sizes; contradictory findings are not deeply unpacked; and recommendations for future research are broad.  Argument structure is present but not yet fully sophisticated, placing the work mid-band (5 within 4-6).

Analysis moves beyond description to link increases in BDNF or SWS to mechanisms of memory consolidation and to evaluate methodological rigour (random assignment, sample size).  Conclusions about intensity-dependent effects and genetic moderation are generally supported by cited data.

However, depth is uneven: statistical effect sizes are not considered, causal language sometimes overreaches the correlational evidence, and integration of studies into a coherent model is limited.  These features align with a solid but not exceptional analytical levelâmiddle of the 4-6 bandâearning 5 marks.

annotations.comment: Indent sub-headings and use leader dots in the table of contents to clarify hierarchy and improve readability for the reader.
The introduction defines adolescence using WHO criteria and cites Azevedo et al., demonstrating excellent subject knowledge and contextual grounding.
The research question refers broadly to âmemory.â Specify which type (e.g., declarative, working) this study addresses to sharpen focus and methodological alignment.
The title âAerobic Exercise and Memory During Adolescenceâ is clear and immediately communicates the topic. This precision in titling helps orient the reader to the research focus effectively.
Presenting the word count (3952) at the start meets formatting requirements and confirms compliance with the 4000-word limit. This demonstrates attention to submission guidelines.
The table of contents includes all major sections, enabling easy navigation and showing a well-structured plan for the investigation.
The opening paragraph on page 4 effectively previews the structure, outlining claims and counterclaims to guide the reader through the argumentâs flow.
The methodology is not described explicitly; add a subsection detailing database searches, keywords, dates, and inclusion/exclusion criteria to increase transparency.
Summarising Jeon et al.âs sample demographics and intervention design shows solid application of primary empirical evidence.
No statistical details (p-values, effect sizes) accompany the reported differences. Include these to strengthen analytical depth and allow evaluation of significance.
The critique of study reliability via ELISA and VOâmax testing is balanced and reflects clear critical evaluation of internal validity.
The text implies cortisol reduction is meaningful despite non-significance. Revise to note that the decrease was not statistically significant.
The explanation of NREM and REM sleep stages is thorough, using accurate terminology like SWS and theta rhythms.
The claim links slow-wave sleep to memory broadly. Clarify which memory type (e.g., declarative vs procedural) is supported by SWS improvements.
Highlighting the studyâs high internal validity through hdEEG combined with PSG demonstrates sound critical appraisal.
The sample size and demographic homogeneity limit generalizability. Discuss how cultural or socioeconomic factors might affect applicability.
The conclusion reinforces how improved sleep architecture supports memory consolidation, tying back to the sleep-exercise link effectively.
Add a brief transition before âCounter Evidenceâ to summarise key support and prepare the reader for the counterclaims.
Introducing exercise intensity as a moderating factor adds nuance and depth to the central argument.
The analysis omits discussion of potential confounders (diet, baseline sleep) in the intensity study. Address these to strengthen evaluation.
The section on genetic influences (Counter Claim 2) incorporates large-scale PNC data, enriching the breadth of evidence.
Discussion of the genetic study omits environmental covariates and geneâenvironment interactions; adding this would deepen critical evaluation.
Specify which heritability model was used (e.g., ACE twin model) to clarify the genetic analysis methodology.
The dense description of three CNB memory tasks could be streamlined with a summary table, improving clarity and reader engagement.
The discussion contrasts lab experiments and observational genetics studies, showing strong critical synthesis of methodological diversity.
Consider adding a conceptual diagram to visualise how exercise-induced BDNF, sleep changes, and genetics interact to influence adolescent memory.
The discussion synthesises environmental and genetic influences well, acknowledging their interplay in adolescent cognitive development.
The discussion notes methodological differences but stops short of integrating variables; explicitly address how to reconcile divergent study designs.
